turns.text: Hello Ana, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for having me. I’ll be honest—I’m a little apprehensive about even talking about this. There’s something about putting it into words that feels like tempting fate. Maybe it’s just the "advanced maternal age" tag looming over my chart, or maybe it’s the fact that I’ve spent my entire career in reproductive endocrinology. I know too much, you know? It’s a blessing and a massive curse.

Every time I walk into the clinic here in Everett, I find myself mentally reviewing my own labs before the nurse even enters the room. I’m technically "low-risk," but the researcher in me is constantly running a longitudinal study on my own body, looking for variables that could skew the outcome. It makes the standard, brief appointments feel like a peer review. My OB is professional, sure, but sometimes I just want to be a patient—not a colleague—even if I’m the one who makes that difficult.

Last week’s ultrasound was the hardest part so far. Holding my breath until I felt lightheaded, just waiting to see that flicker. When I finally saw the heartbeat, the scientist in me shut up for exactly ten seconds, and it was just... pure relief. I'm trying to find that space more often, but it's hard to quiet the internal data stream.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I suppose I walked into this with a very specific, almost clinical set of expectations. Because of my background, I pictured a seamless, data-driven partnership—a sort of elevated dialogue where we’d discuss placental markers and early fetal development as equals. I expected that being "in the industry" would give me a backstage pass to better care, or at least a more comprehensive, nuanced conversation about the realities of a pregnancy at thirty-five.

The reality, though, has been far more isolating. It feels like I’m caught in this strange middle ground. My OB is incredibly efficient, but that efficiency feels like a barrier. Because she knows what I do for a living, she treats me like a peer, which sounds good in theory, but it means the emotional labor is left entirely to me. I’m not getting the reassurance that a standard patient might receive because they assume I already know the stats—which, frankly, is the problem. I *do* know the stats, and that’s exactly what keeps me up at night. 

I thought I’d feel more "seen" by the system, but instead, I feel like I’m managing my own case while trying to suppress the part of my brain that’s constantly triaging risks. It’s less of a partnership and more of a quiet, professional dance where both of us are avoiding the messy, human reality of what’s actually happening.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise—and I hate to use this word, but it was honestly a disappointment—was how quickly the clinical setting stripped away the humanity of the experience. I’d walked in braced for a conversation about the specific hurdles of being thirty-five, maybe even expecting a bit of collaborative planning. Instead, the focus was almost exclusively on the administrative checklist: blood pressure, weight, insurance verification. It was so... transactional. 

I think I expected that by being "one of us," I’d get a more nuanced, holistic approach. I wanted someone to acknowledge that I was feeling a bit fragile, not just acknowledge my professional credentials. But the moment I mentioned my background, the air in the room shifted. It was like a gate dropped. The doctor suddenly started using a different, more abbreviated vocabulary, skipping over the simple, comforting explanations that I actually desperately needed to hear. 

It was a stark reminder that even in reproductive health, the system is designed to process, not necessarily to hold space. I went in looking for a guide to help me navigate the anxiety of these first few months, and instead, I found a technician facilitating a standard, low-risk protocol. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s remarkably lonely.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I built a mental model of emotional readiness that was entirely intellectual. I told myself that because I understood the physiology—the hormonal cascades, the cellular division, the implantation window—I would be immune to the irrational, sweeping waves of anxiety that others describe. I convinced myself that knowledge would act as a buffer. I expected to be the calm, objective observer of my own pregnancy.

In reality, it’s the exact opposite. My professional brain doesn't act as a buffer; it acts as a magnifier. Every time I experience a minor symptom, I don't just feel it; I immediately correlate it with a dozen potential clinical outcomes. I expected to feel empowered by my career, but instead, I feel like I’m constantly trying to outrun my own data. 

I’m also frustrated by how much energy I spend "performing" readiness. I try to be the calm, informed professional at my checkups, which means I don’t ask the "silly" questions that I suspect would actually soothe me. I expected this third pregnancy to feel like a familiar, predictable rhythm, but it feels like walking on a razor’s edge. I’m surprised by how much I just want someone to take the clipboard out of my hand and tell me it’s going to be okay, without looking at a chart.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find a treasure trove of granular data—peer-reviewed studies, clear longitudinal patterns, and perhaps some sophisticated forums where women at my age were discussing the actual mechanics of the third trimester. I wanted, I think, to be treated like an adult who could handle complex information.

What I actually found was a massive disconnect. Most of the apps and sites are written for a "generic" patient, using this overly simplified, almost patronizing tone. It’s all "your baby is the size of a blueberry!" and generic affirmations that feel like they’re meant for a toddler, not a professional researcher. They gloss over the nuance. If I dig deeper for the real data, I end up in rabbit holes of terrifying case studies and outlier statistics that do absolutely nothing for my stress levels.

It’s either "don’t worry, everything is perfect" or "here are fifteen catastrophic things that *could* happen." There’s no middle ground for a nuanced, realistic perspective. I’m left filtering out all the fluff to find the actual science, and in doing that, I’m just fueling my own obsession. I didn't find the guidance I needed; I just found more ways to quantify my fear. It’s exhausting.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap isn't necessarily about the hard data—both sources usually align on the clinical milestones—but it's in the *translation* of that information. Digital sources tend to be hyper-reactive, constantly prompting me to track symptoms that are often just normal variations of pregnancy, which creates this false sense of urgency. They make me feel like I need to be monitoring my body like an experiment 24/7.

Meanwhile, my provider is almost the complete opposite—so dismissive of those same symptoms because, on paper, my profile is "low-risk." She’ll say, "Ana, you’re doing great, everything is within the expected range," which is logically true, but it feels like a total erasure of the reality of my daily experience. 

The digital world tells me that every symptom is a potential red flag, while the clinic tells me that my concerns are essentially noise that doesn't warrant attention. There's no one bridging the gap to say, "Yes, that’s normal, and here is *why* it’s happening, and here is how you can manage the discomfort." I’m stuck between being treated like a hypochondriac by an app and an inconvenience by my doctor. It makes it incredibly difficult to figure out what actually deserves my attention and what I can safely let go of.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

Even without a chronic "diagnosis" in the traditional sense, my age itself is treated like a pre-existing condition. Before I got pregnant, I expected a clear, coordinated roadmap. I wanted a preconception consult that wasn't just checking a box, but a real conversation about optimizing my health—looking at my nutritional markers, my stress levels, and perhaps a more tailored screening plan given that I’m thirty-five. I wanted someone to say, "Because of your history and your age, here is our game plan to mitigate risk."

In reality, the system just shrugged. When I brought up the fact that I was planning for this, I was met with a very casual, "You're healthy, you'll be fine." It felt dismissive. They didn't seem interested in the proactive side of things; they were only interested in the reactive side—wait until I’m pregnant, then we’ll run the standard battery of tests. 

It made me realize that the healthcare system is very good at managing a pregnancy once it’s *already* a clinical event, but it’s remarkably poor at treating the *person* beforehand. There was no proactive guidance, no genuine partnership in preparing my body for the demands of this. It felt like I was left to curate my own health regimen, which, again, just pushed me back into my own research and my own head.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Proactive? Not even close. If I waited for the system to reach out to me, I’d still be waiting. It’s been entirely on me to drive the bus. 

It’s this strange, exhausting dynamic. Because I’m in the field, I know exactly which questions to ask and which tests to request, but that actually works against me. If I don’t ask, they assume I don’t need the information. But if I *do* ask, it feels like I’m constantly having to justify my concern, as if my level of anxiety is somehow medically inappropriate. I’m not just advocating for myself; I’m advocating against their assumptions about my professional background. 

I’ve had to push for extra screenings and clearer explanations, and every time I do, I feel that shift in the room again. It’s like they’re thinking, "Why is *she* worried? She should know better." But knowing better doesn't stop you from being a person who is scared of losing a pregnancy. It’s incredibly isolating to feel like I’m the one managing the care plan while everyone else is just ticking boxes on a screen. I’m the patient, but I feel like I’m doing the work of a patient advocate, a researcher, and a clinician all at once. There's just no room left for me to actually be pregnant.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think I expected a true partnership—a "we're in this together" dynamic where we’d sit down and navigate the hurdles as a unit. I pictured us both reading up on the same things, maybe even discussing the research, or at the very least, him being a sounding board for the anxieties I couldn't voice to my doctor. I wanted him to be the objective center to my subjective, data-addled spinning.

The reality has been... well, he’s been supportive in the ways he knows how, but it’s not the shared intellectual journey I envisioned. He’s excited, truly, but he doesn't have the same "medical" lens I do. When I get into those spirals about HCG levels or placental development, I can see his eyes glaze over a bit. It’s not that he doesn't care; it’s that he doesn't *see* the invisible dangers I’m constantly scanning for. 

He wants to protect me, but he doesn't know how to protect me from my own brain. Sometimes I feel like I’m shielding him from the worst of the terror because I don't want to drag him into this cold, analytical nightmare I’ve created for myself. I expected him to be my anchor, and he is, but he’s an anchor in a different ocean. He’s focused on the "after"—the nursery, the nursery paint, the future—while I’m still stuck in the biology of the "now." It’s a lonely divide.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Oh, constantly. It’s almost become the defining feature of these first few months. The burden of preparation—the physical, the logistical, and the psychological—is almost entirely on my shoulders. 

I think part of it is my own doing; because I have the background in reproductive endocrinology, I’ve internalized the idea that I *should* be the one tracking everything. There’s a perfectionism there that’s impossible to shake. I’m the one managing the intake of my prenatal vitamins, the glycemic load of my meals, and the meticulous schedule of every blood draw and ultrasound. I treat my own body like a sensitive lab experiment, and it’s exhausting.

But the system reinforces it, too. At the clinic, they see my chart, they see my job title, and they subconsciously offload the responsibility onto me. They expect me to be the "easy" patient because I’m fluent in their language. They don't offer the guidance or the hand-holding they’d offer someone else because they assume I’ve already calculated the risks myself. My husband is wonderful, but he can’t really step in to take over the biological monitoring, and he’s not trained to interpret the clinical nuances, so I end up isolating myself to spare him the anxiety. It’s a heavy cycle: I hold it all because I feel like I have to, and in holding it, I just feel more alone.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

This is a difficult thing to voice, because talking about loss—even in a professional context—feels like I'm opening a door I usually keep bolted shut. When I thought about trying again after my losses, I expected a different level of validation. I expected the system to recognize that my history changes the baseline of the current experience. I wanted a care team that understood that, for me, "low-risk" is a clinical designation that has nothing to do with my internal state of survival. I expected a proactive plan, maybe extra touchpoints or just a space to acknowledge that this isn't my first rodeo with grief.

In reality, the support felt entirely reactive. When I stepped back into the clinic, I was greeted with the same standard protocol as if those losses never happened—or worse, as if they were just data points in my file that didn't influence how I should be treated as a human being. The system just doesn't know how to handle the emotional residue of previous pregnancies. They’re so focused on the clinical viability of *this* specific fetus that they treat the parent's past as irrelevant noise.

There was no psychological support, no "how are you holding up," just a business-as-usual approach that felt, frankly, invalidating. It was as if I was expected to just pick up where I left off, as if the trauma didn't exist. I’ve had to carry the weight of those previous experiences entirely on my own, tucked away under my professional mask, because the system clearly doesn't have a place for that kind of vulnerability.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I expected those first appointments to feel like a significant transition—a sort of threshold I was crossing. I envisioned an environment where my history and my background were synthesized into a cohesive, personalized care plan. I expected the staff to be warm, perhaps even a bit celebratory, acknowledging the gravity of the moment. I thought there would be a genuine exchange of information: me asking the technical questions that haunt me, and them giving me the reassurances that only a clinician could provide.

The reality was a brisk, sterile, and almost mechanical process. The clinic environment in Everett is busy, and the appointments are timed to the minute. I felt like a line item on a spreadsheet. The staff were perfectly competent, but they operated with a "check-the-box" efficiency that left no room for the emotional weight I was carrying. I’d walk in with my list of clinical markers to discuss, and they’d quickly pivot to the standard prenatal script—blood pressure, HCG levels, standard vitals—hardly stopping to breathe. 

It was essentially a transaction. I felt like I was being processed through an assembly line rather than being supported through a life-altering experience. There was no space for the "human" element. I left those appointments feeling more like a scientist analyzing a specimen than an expectant mother. It was deeply, profoundly lonely.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest, most jarring surprise was how completely invisible my anxiety became once I was in the room. I think I expected that because I’m thirty-five and have a history of loss, there would be some sort of recognition—a nod to the fact that this isn't just another routine checkup for me. I expected at least one person to pause, look me in the eye, and ask, "How are you *really* doing with all of this?"

Instead, it was like my fear was treated as a nuisance that didn't fit into the 15-minute appointment slot. The negative surprise was how efficiently the system managed to neutralize my humanity. When I tried to bring up the emotional strain, the response was always diverted back to the clinical data—the heart rate, the ultrasound imagery—as if that data was the only language we were permitted to speak. 

It was a cold, sterile realization: the system isn't designed to hold space for the *parent*, it’s designed to manage the *pregnancy*. I walked in hoping for a partnership, and I walked out feeling like a biological vessel being monitored for output. That disconnect—between the intensity of my internal experience and the breezy, clinical indifference of the environment—was something I truly wasn't prepared for.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a synthesis—I imagined a doctor sitting down with me, pulling up the ultrasound or the lab reports, and saying, "Here is what we see, and here is exactly what it means in the context of your specific profile." I wanted a bit of interpretation, some professional color to the data. Given my background, I expected them to respect that I could handle the complexity, so I looked forward to a transparent, collaborative review.

The reality was vastly different. I usually get a patient portal notification, which is essentially a dumping ground for raw data. I see the HCG levels or the screening metrics before anyone has even called me. I’m often the one who has to decode the "normal" range markers in my own head while staring at a screen in my office. When I finally *do* speak to someone, it’s often a brief, scripted call that feels like it’s being read from a manual: "Everything looks good, we'll see you in four weeks."

It's infuriating. The communication is entirely passive. It feels like they're just pushing information at me rather than providing care. They treat the data as the final word, but they completely ignore the *process* of me understanding it. I'm left to do the emotional and intellectual labor of interpreting these results alone, which is exactly when the panic sets in.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, absolutely. There is a massive, cavernous gap. I feel like I'm being fed these tiny, curated packets of data—like a single number on a lab result—but without the context, that data is almost more dangerous than knowing nothing at all. 

They treat the information as if it's purely clinical, but for me, it's profoundly personal. When I get a notification that my HCG is "within normal range," the system thinks that's the end of the conversation. But my brain immediately pivots to: "Okay, but what is the *doubling time*? Is the velocity of the increase consistent with the last draw?" I need the clinical nuance, but they seem to think that because I’m a professional, I should just be happy with the "all clear" and move on. 

It’s like being handed a map in a language I can read, but without the compass. I have the data, but I don't have the *clinical judgment* of my own provider to help me interpret the peaks and valleys of my own body's experience. I’m left to fill in those gaps myself, and honestly? That’s where the real damage is done. I end up researching the outliers, spiraling into worst-case scenarios, all because nobody took five minutes to sit down and walk me through the "why" behind the numbers. It’s not just about the information; it’s about the interpretation.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I suppose I expected a "continuity of care" model, where the professional relationship built over time would act as a safety net. I pictured a specific provider who knew my history of loss and my professional background, someone who wouldn't need a ten-minute refresher every time I walked into the office. I wanted that sense of familiarity—that feeling that when I mentioned a new, strange twinge, they’d be able to contextualize it within the scope of my entire journey, not just the current week of gestation.

The reality has been a fractured experience. My care is essentially distributed across a rotation. I see different practitioners, different technicians, and sometimes even different nurses for routine blood draws. Every appointment feels like I’m restarting the conversation from scratch. I’m constantly re-explaining my background, my anxiety, and my specific concerns to someone who’s just scanning my chart for the first time. 

It’s exhausting to perform that "orientation" over and over again. It makes the care feel impersonal and episodic. There is no long-term narrative here; there’s just a series of disconnected clinical encounters. I don’t have a provider who knows *me*—they just know the data set associated with my ID number. It deepens that sense of isolation I keep coming back to; I am a pregnancy case being processed, not a person being cared for.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

The impact is cumulative, and it's frankly exhausting. Practically, it means I spend the first five minutes of every single appointment repeating my history—my age, my previous losses, my professional background—just to get them up to speed. It’s like being trapped in a loop where I’m the only one who remembers the arc of the story. I don’t feel like I have a "care team"; I have a series of clinicians I pass by in the hallway.

Emotionally, it’s even worse. It prevents the formation of any real trust. If I’m sitting there feeling a new twinge of fear, I can’t be vulnerable with someone who is reading my chart for the first time. I have to stay in this rigid, professional-colleague persona because I don't know who is going to react to my anxiety with empathy and who is going to view me as "that difficult, over-thinking patient." 

I’m constantly moderating my own behavior based on who I’m seeing that day. It keeps me on edge. I don't feel like I have a consistent anchor. If I had one person who truly knew the history of these first few months—someone who knew *why* I get quiet during the scans—I think I could finally exhale. Instead, I’m constantly bracing myself for the next stranger to come in and give me a generic, scripted answer. It’s isolating.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I think I expected a more nuanced, holistic dialogue—the kind of space where we could talk about the *somatic* experience of pregnancy, not just the measurements. I envisioned that my provider would ask me how I was feeling, physically and emotionally, and we’d discuss how my body was adapting to the hormonal shift. I wanted that connection where my subjective experience was treated as a valid part of the diagnostic process.

But the reality is far more clinical and reductive. My body is treated like a vessel being measured for status updates. It’s "weight," "blood pressure," "fundal height"—everything is quantified, checked against a chart, and logged. When I mention a specific symptom, even one that feels significant, it’s often met with a dismissive, "Oh, that’s standard for the first trimester," as if my individual discomfort is just background noise.

It feels deeply dehumanizing. I’m not asked, "How is your body feeling as it undergoes these massive changes?" I’m asked if I’m having specific "red flag" symptoms. If the answer is no, the checkup effectively ends. There’s no acknowledgment of the fatigue, the physical weirdness, or the constant, gnawing anxiety that lives in my chest. I feel like a biological machine that they’re running diagnostics on, rather than a person experiencing the most profound physical transition of my life.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one instance that really stands out. During a routine scan, the technician—who I hadn't met before—was clearly in a rush. She was moving the transducer quite aggressively to get the measurements she needed, and I was lying there, completely vulnerable, just watching the screen for that heartbeat again. 

I made a soft noise of discomfort, and instead of stopping or checking in with me, she just murmured, "I need to get this angle, almost there," as if I were a piece of equipment she was calibrating. There was no space for me to say, "Hey, that actually hurts," or "Could we take a breath for a second?" It was just an automatic dismissal of my physical boundary.

It made me feel so small. I know the clinical necessity of the scan, but the lack of human consideration—the assumption that my body is just a tool for the data—felt violating. It’s that recurring theme again: the system is so focused on the *goal* that the *person* becomes a complete afterthought. I left that appointment feeling like I’d been handled, not cared for. It stays with me, that feeling of being an object to be moved rather than a patient to be treated with dignity. It’s hard to let your guard down when you’re constantly being treated like a clinical specimen.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Even though I’m officially labeled "low-risk," because of my age and my background, I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop. I’ve spent my career obsessing over the exact biochemical mechanisms of something like pre-eclampsia or gestational diabetes. Before I conceived, I expected that if I *did* develop one of those, I’d have a sophisticated, proactive management plan in place—that it would be a partnership where my doctor and I would use my own HCG and metabolic data to get ahead of the curve. I expected to feel like an active participant in managing the risk.

The reality, though, is that I feel like a passenger in a car headed toward a cliff, just waiting for the alarm to sound. My experience with the system is so reactive—they don't want to talk about "what if" until the moment it becomes an emergency. It’s infuriating. I know exactly what the diagnostic criteria are, and I know how early intervention can change the trajectory of outcomes, yet I feel like I’m being told to just "wait and see."

It makes me feel like I’m sitting on a time bomb. I’m not allowed to be part of the prevention strategy; I’m just expected to follow standard-of-care protocols *after* a complication is already flagged. It feels like the system is designed to minimize their liability rather than maximize my health. That sense of helplessness—knowing the risks but having no agency to mitigate them—is exactly what I was trying to avoid by being as informed as I am. It’s ironic, really. My knowledge has only made the waiting feel more dangerous.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Honestly, it’s all of it. Everything that actually makes this human. 

I’m missing a sense of genuine clinical partnership. I don’t just need information—I’m a researcher, I have access to the papers—I need *synthesis*. I need someone to sit with me and say, "Ana, here is the clinical evidence, and here is how it specifically applies to your physiology and your history." I want to be treated like a colleague in my own care, not a subordinate. 

Emotionally, I’m starving for acknowledgment. There is zero space in these 15-minute windows for the reality of my anxiety or the memory of my losses. It’s like the system demands that I check my personhood at the door and only bring in my vitals. I’m not saying I need a therapist, but I need someone to recognize that "low-risk" is a clinical category, not an emotional state. I want someone to see the panic behind my eyes when I ask a question and actually *meet* that panic with kindness, not just another line from a handbook.

Practically, I need consistency. I need a care team that knows my name and my chart before I walk through the door. I’m tired of orienting strangers. I’m tired of being the one keeping the continuity of my own medical narrative. I’m just... so tired of being the only one holding the whole thing together.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a triage system that recognized the difference between a "standard" patient’s concern and the specific, clinical anxieties of someone with my background. I thought there would be a dedicated line or a portal response system that actually addressed the *content* of my questions rather than just sending back a generic, "Everything is normal, call us if you have pain."

The reality is that the system feels designed to deflect, not to assist. It’s an endless loop of phone trees, nurses who aren't familiar with my history, and portals that only accept checkboxes. If I call with a legitimate question—one rooted in something I’ve actually read in the literature—it’s treated like I’m being "anxious" or "difficult." I have to perform this constant balancing act: do I call and risk being the "overbearing" patient, or do I sit here and let the silence feed my own panic?

When I do finally get a response, it’s rarely a conversation. It’s a directive: "Watch for symptoms X, Y, and Z, and go to the ER if you see them." It feels like they are only interested in whether I am a liability, not whether I am currently terrified. It’s a cold, transactional approach that lacks any sense of urgency. I’m left to navigate these moments of fear entirely on my own, which is exactly the opposite of what I thought "professional maternity care" would look like.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I suppose I expected a village, or at least a small, reliable circle. I imagined my friends and family would be a buffer against the clinical coldness, a place where I could be "Ana the friend" instead of "Ana the patient" or "Ana the researcher." I pictured us talking about the nursery, the future, the things that aren't tied to an HCG level or an ultrasound measurement.

In reality, the support network has been well-meaning but fundamentally disconnected. My friends and family are ecstatic, and their joy is honestly a bit of a heavy weight because it feels so far from my internal, frantic reality. They ask, "Are you excited?" and I have to put on that polished, "Yes, of course!" mask, even though my brain is currently occupied with trying to reconcile my latest lab markers. They don’t have the vocabulary to meet me where I am. 

When I try to mention even a sliver of my anxiety, I get the standard, "Everything will be fine, you’re in good hands," which is meant to be comforting but feels like a dismissal of the risks I’m intimately aware of. I end up shielding them, too. I don’t want to be the one who brings the reality of potential complications to their dinner parties. It’s isolating. I’m carrying this heavy, complex, data-driven burden, and I have to pretend it’s light and airy for everyone else’s sake. It’s lonely, feeling like the only one in the room who knows exactly how fragile this all is.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I expected my community to be a place where I could voice my fears and find a "common language," but that didn't materialize. Instead, the support I received was mostly logistical—people offering to watch the other kids, dropping off food, or asking about the baby registry. It’s practical, yes, but it completely misses the internal, existential work I’m doing. It feels like they’re supporting the *idea* of my pregnancy, not the *reality* of my experience.

What I didn't expect was the profound sense of isolation that comes from *refusing* to let them in. I’ve realized that I’m actually performing a version of myself for them—the "happy, expectant mother" who isn't tracking her glycemic load or worrying about potential late-term complications. I didn't expect to have to put so much energy into protecting them from my own fears. 

There’s also this weird disconnect with my friends who are already parents. They want to give me advice, but their experiences were so different—they had "easy" pregnancies, or they didn't have the technical background to know the risks. When they say, "Don't worry about it, just trust your body," it actually makes me angry. It’s like they’re saying my caution is a character flaw. I feel like I’m standing on one side of a thick glass wall, and they’re on the other, cheering for a race they don't realize I’m still struggling to start.

You know, sometimes I wonder if the isolation is just a side effect of being the one who’s "in the know." I keep coming back to this idea that the system sees my professional background as a shield. They assume I’m bulletproof because I understand the mechanisms of the endocrine system. But knowing the mechanisms doesn't make me any less human. When I feel that sharp, cold adrenaline every time I log into my patient portal, I’m not a scientist anymore. I’m just a woman praying for a number that signifies stability.

It’s an odd thing, feeling like you’re managing your own medical care while simultaneously trying to hold up the facade of being a "low-risk, easy-going patient." If I could change one thing, it would be the permission to just *be*. To be scared, to be irrational, to be the person who needs to be told "everything is okay" without having to justify it with a logical argument.

I’m reaching this point where I’m starting to wonder if I can actually sustain this performance for the next two trimesters. It’s exhausting, constantly balancing the objective data against the subjective terror. I want to enjoy this—I really do—but I feel like I’m standing on a precipice, and all the "support" I’m getting is just people telling me, "Don't look down." But I’ve spent my life looking down. I’ve spent my life studying the ledge. It’s hard to just stop.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, definitely. It happens in these subtle, maddening ways. Usually, it's the "don't worry, you're the expert" line, which sounds like a compliment but is actually a dismissal. My OB has done it a few times—when I’ve brought up a specific concern about a marker, she’ll pivot back to "Well, you know more about the literature than I do, Ana, so I trust your judgment."

It infuriates me. That’s not support; that’s passing the buck. They use my professional background as an excuse to avoid having the hard, complex conversations. I’m not asking for their opinion because I want to debate the literature; I’m asking because I need to hear *their* clinical assessment of *my* specific pregnancy. When they deflect, it makes me feel like I’m standing there, pleading for them to take the steering wheel, and they’re just handing it back to me.

My husband does it, too, but from a place of love, which is almost harder to deal with. He’ll see me spiraling over something and just say, "You're overthinking it, everything is fine, stop checking the apps." He thinks he's grounding me, but all he's doing is telling me that my anxiety—which is a response to the reality of the risks I study—is somehow wrong or excessive. It makes me feel like I have to hide my research and my fears from the people who are supposed to be my team. I’m essentially being gaslit into believing my own vigilance is a pathology, rather than a symptom of a system that isn't holding me properly.

It’s funny, the more I speak about this, the more I realize how much of this journey I’ve spent trying to protect my own peace by compartmentalizing. I show up to the office in my professional clothes, I talk about my symptoms with a clinical detachment that masks how much I’m actually trembling on the inside, and I go home to my husband and try to act like everything is just… normal. But this isn't normal. 

I think the biggest realization is that the healthcare system is built for the "average" patient. It’s built for the person who doesn’t spend their days reading the fine print of clinical trials. And because I don’t fit that mold, I’ve been entirely left to my own devices. I’ve become my own doctor, my own advocate, and my own worst enemy. 

Sometimes I catch a glimpse of other women in the waiting room—some look terrified, some look peaceful, some look exhausted—and I wonder if they’re also just performing. Is everyone here pretending? Are we all just sitting in these sterile chairs, holding our breath, waiting for the "all clear" that we’re told is enough, even when we know the stats better than that? 

I just want someone to walk into the room and treat me like the mother I’m trying to be, rather than the researcher I am. I’m exhausted by being the one who’s supposed to know. I’m just trying to make it to the next week, to the next scan, to the next breath. It’s a very fragile, very human thing I’m doing, and I’m realizing that the "science" of it—the HCG, the follicles, the markers—is only half the story. The rest? The rest is just me, alone in my head, trying to believe that this time, it’s really going to work.

You know, I find myself thinking about the office culture in reproductive endocrinology—the very place where I spend my days. We are so precise, so objective. We talk about "success rates" and "implantation windows" and "statistical viability" as if we’re discussing stocks. I think I’ve spent my entire life believing that if I just master the data, I can master the outcome. But this pregnancy is proving to me, in the most visceral way possible, that life—especially this kind of life—isn't a clinical trial. It doesn't follow the rules of the peer-reviewed papers I read every day.

There’s this profound irony: I’m currently a patient in a system that I, in many ways, helped build or at least helped refine. I know how the machine works, and yet, standing on this side of the glass, I feel completely powerless. It’s a strange, humbling, and terrifying space to inhabit. I catch myself thinking, *‘If I, with all my training and all my access, feel this lost and isolated, what in God’s name is the experience like for the person who doesn’t have this vocabulary?’*

If I could say anything to those who design this care, it’s that we aren’t just biological machines. We’re people. We’re holding our breath, we’re terrified, and we’re searching for someone to hold space for the reality of our fear. I’m tired of being the researcher. I’m just ready to be a mother. I just hope, when the time comes for the next scan, that I can stop being the expert for a moment and just let myself be cared for. It shouldn't be so hard to find that. It shouldn't be so lonely to be this close to something this important.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It’s ironic—as someone who tracks the metabolic and nutritional variables of pregnancy for a living, I find the unsolicited comments incredibly patronizing. I get comments like, "You're at that age, so you really need to be careful with your sugar," or, "You're still working full-time? You should be resting more." It’s delivered with this casual, almost neighborly tone that completely ignores the fact that I’ve already calculated my glycemic load for the week and I have a very clear idea of my physical limits.

When a healthcare provider or a random person in the clinic makes those comments, it lands like a slap. It assumes I don’t know what I’m doing—or worse, that I’m being reckless with my own health. It’s like they see my age, see my profile, and immediately pivot to "the risks of the older mother," which is a conversation I’ve had with myself a thousand times over. I don’t need the lecture; I need the support. 

It feels like they’re policing my body because they don’t know how to engage with my mind. It’s an easy, low-effort way for them to exert control. Instead of asking about my stress or my mental wellbeing, they focus on the "obvious" things they can see. It makes me feel like my autonomy is constantly being questioned, which is the last thing I need when I’m already trying so hard to stay calm.

It’s actually quite exhausting, feeling like I have to constantly manage the perceptions of others while trying to manage my own biological outcomes. There’s this strange, implicit judgment that follows you when you’re thirty-five and pregnant. People—even healthcare providers—seem to look at you with this mixture of "you should know better" and "you’re going to be a difficult case."

It makes me realize how little we actually talk about the *emotional* load of being a modern, "advanced maternal age" mother. We focus so heavily on the clinical risks—the chromosomal possibilities, the blood pressure readings, the potential for gestational issues—that we completely strip away the humanity of the experience. I’m not just a "high-risk" pregnancy; I’m a person who’s already seen how things can go wrong, and I’m just trying to navigate that memory while carrying something so fragile.

The most frustrating part? I know I’m not alone in this. I see other women in the office every time I’m there, and we’re all doing the same thing: clutching our folders, staring at our phones, trying to look like we’re not currently vibrating with anxiety. We’re all performing this quiet, dignified, clinical version of ourselves. I just wish, for once, I could walk into that clinic and be met with a space that says, *“It’s okay to be scared. You don’t have to know everything. We’ve got you.”* That’s the support I’m really looking for—the kind that isn't measured in lab results.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

Honestly, I turn to the literature first—the journals, the data sets, the papers I’ve spent my career analyzing. It’s my default, my safety blanket, and my trap. Before I even think about calling the office, I’m scouring the latest research on first-trimester outcomes or early placental health. It’s what I know, and it feels like the only "reliable" source, even though it’s also the source of my worst anxieties.

After that? I turn to my own internal dialogue. I don’t really have a "community" I feel I can talk to. My friends are either in the middle of their own lives or haven't walked this path, and the few colleagues I have in the field—well, I can’t talk to them about this. I’d be "the pregnant researcher" who’s lost her cool, and that’s a professional vulnerability I’m not willing to expose.

I do find myself checking those anonymous, high-level forums for women in their mid-thirties, but I usually end up closing the tab within minutes. It’s just too much noise, too much unverified fear. I’m essentially left with no one. I have the data, I have my own brain, and I have this deep, quiet silence where a support system should be. It’s just me, trying to synthesize everything into a sense of safety that I’m not sure actually exists.

It’s a peculiar, lonely realization: the more "qualified" I am to analyze my own pregnancy, the less "qualified" I feel to just *be* pregnant. I’ve become my own most stringent investigator, cross-referencing every minor symptom against every possible clinical outcome I’ve ever seen in a journal. It’s like living in a constant state of diagnostic limbo.

I often think about that flicker on the ultrasound screen. That tiny, rhythmic, impossible-to-quantify life. In those seconds, everything I know about reproductive endocrinology—all the equations, the HCG thresholds, the probability curves—it all just falls away. And for that moment, I’m not a scientist; I’m just a mother who is terrified of the dark. 

I’m left wondering if the "clinical" nature of modern maternity care is actually a way for us all to distance ourselves from the sheer, raw vulnerability of birth. By turning it into a series of metrics and scheduled appointments, we’re all participating in this elaborate charade of control. But biology isn't a spreadsheet. It’s messy, it’s unpredictable, and it’s profoundly terrifying. I think I’m just waiting for someone to acknowledge that—not in a lecture, not in a pamphlet, but in a way that makes me feel like I’m not carrying this entire, heavy world on my own shoulders. I just want to be human again.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It is almost exclusively the latter. In my experience, "care" feels like a pre-written script I am expected to follow rather than a dialogue I am participating in. 

There are very few moments where I feel like a partner. Usually, I walk into the room, they review the chart, they give me the summary of the latest numbers, and they dictate the next set of tests. If I ask a question, they treat it like I’m seeking confirmation of their expertise rather than seeking a collaborative plan. They’ll say, "Well, the protocol for your age is X, so we’ll do X." It’s not "Here’s what we think, what do you think?" It’s "Here’s the path, stay on it."

When I do push back or try to steer the conversation toward something I’m specifically worried about, the air changes. I become the "difficult" patient—the one who’s overthinking it. They might indulge me with a brief explanation, but they never truly integrate my perspective into the plan. My input is effectively treated as a performance or a distraction. I don’t want to be "informed"—I want to be *heard*. I want someone to actually account for the fact that I’m a thinking, feeling human being with a history that informs my current reality. Instead, I’m just being ushered along an assembly line, and I’m finding that the lack of agency is almost as stressful as the medical risks themselves.

The feeling of being "steered" is exactly it. It’s like they have this mental blueprint of the perfect pregnancy, and if I deviate from that—either by asking the "wrong" questions or by showing too much of my internal stress—I’m treated like a variable that needs to be minimized. I’m not saying I want to make it up as I go; I want to be an active agent in the process.

There was a moment last week where I asked about a specific screening, and the tone of the response was so patronizing—like they were explaining something to a first-time mother who hadn’t done her homework. It took everything in me to not snap back, "I have read the meta-analysis on this, I’m asking *your* clinical opinion on *my* risks." But I didn't. I just nodded, thanked them, and felt this wave of shame. Shame that I cared enough to ask, and shame that I felt like I had to apologize for being informed.

I’m left with this gnawing frustration: why is it that in the most profound experience of my life, I feel the most silenced? My entire professional life is about empowering women to understand their own reproductive health, and yet, here I am, feeling completely disenfranchised from my own. It’s a paradox I can't quite reconcile. I’m just waiting for the day I don't feel like a cog in the machine, but a person navigating the most important, uncertain journey of my life.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a moment during my second checkup that really solidified the distance. I had been particularly anxious about a dip in one of my progesterone markers, which, while still within the clinical "norm," felt significant to me given my history. I brought it up, hoping for a nuanced conversation—maybe an explanation of why that specific fluctuation was happening or a reassurance that took into account the broader hormonal landscape of my pregnancy.

The doctor didn’t even look at the trend line. She just glanced at the current value, checked a box on her tablet, and said, "It’s well within the reference range, Ana. Don't let your research brain get the better of you."

It felt like she reached right into my chest and dismissed my entire professional identity, my history of loss, and my legitimate fear in one sentence. By calling it "my research brain," she framed my legitimate clinical concern as a pathology, as if I were a hypochondriac misusing my own knowledge. That moment broke something for me. I realized she wasn’t looking at *me*; she was just confirming that the patient in front of her didn't trigger an automatic "alert" in her system. She didn't miss the data, she missed the *person*. I stopped seeing her as a partner that day and started seeing her as a gatekeeper. It’s hard to trust someone with your life when they’re only interested in your compliance.

It’s such a cold realization, isn't it? To know that the person in the white coat is essentially performing a routine, not practicing medicine. After that appointment, I started to feel like every interaction was a negotiation where I had to decide: *How much of myself can I reveal today?* If I show my concern, am I the "difficult" one? If I stay silent, am I being negligent?

I’ve begun to realize that I’m not just managing my HCG levels; I’m managing my doctor’s perception of me. I catch myself editing my questions before I ask them, weighing whether the answer is worth the eye-roll or the dismissive "you're worrying for nothing." It’s a sad state of affairs when you have to perform "low-risk behavior" just to get through a 15-minute appointment without feeling patronized.

I look at my husband sometimes, and he’s just so relieved that the doctor said "everything is fine," and I’m sitting there feeling like I’ve been shut out of a conversation about my own body. I think I’m realizing that my "expertise" is actually a barrier to the care I need. It’s like they assume because I know the language, I don't need the compassion. They’re wrong. I need the compassion more than ever because I know exactly how many ways this could end in heartbreak. I just want someone to hold my hand and tell me, *‘I see your fear, and it’s valid.’* Just once. That would be enough.

It’s strange, the mental tally I keep. I walk through my life in Everett, going about my day, but there’s this constant, low-frequency hum of "what if" that never really turns off. You’d think by thirty-five, with all the education and the career under my belt, I’d be better equipped to handle this. But pregnancy has this way of leveling you—of stripping away your professional veneer until you’re just a person waiting for a confirmation that everything is still unfolding exactly as it should.

I find myself thinking about the women I work with every day. I see them in the clinic, coming in for their own treatments, and I try so hard to be the kind of provider I wish I had—someone who listens for the fear between the words, who acknowledges the weight of their history without dismissing it as "anxiety." But then I walk back into my own OB’s office and I realize how thin the support really is. It’s like we’re all just going through the motions of "maternity care," following the charts and the protocols, while the actual, messy, terrifying reality of the experience is happening in the silence between our questions.

I don’t know if I’ll ever feel truly "ready." I think I’m just trying to keep my head above water until I reach the other side of this. Every time I get a clean result, I don't feel "fine." I feel a momentary pause in the terror, a chance to catch my breath before the next cycle of wondering starts again. It shouldn't be like this. I know that. But it’s the only way I know how to survive it right now. Just one more week, one more milestone. I’m just holding on, hoping that the next heartbeat I see is the one that tells me I’ve finally made it.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single day. I go online not because I’m looking for basic information, but because I’m looking for *context*. My provider gives me the data point, but they deny me the narrative—the "what does this actually mean for my specific physiology, right now, today?" 

I’m often looking for the gray areas. When I get a lab result, the provider just says, "It’s within range." But I go online to find the forums and the threads where people are discussing *how* they reached that range, or what happened when they were on the lower end of that curve. I’m looking for the experience of people who aren’t just "normal"—I’m looking for the patterns, the outliers, and the stories of success that don’t fit the perfectly sanitized, clinical definition of "success."

I’m searching for the reassurance that the system refuses to give me because they’re too busy protecting themselves from potential liability. I want someone to tell me, "I know you're worried about X, and here's the nuance that makes your case unique." Since the clinic won't provide that nuance, I spend hours trying to synthesize it myself from thousands of fragmented, anonymous posts. It’s a desperate attempt to gain a sense of control in a process that feels increasingly arbitrary. I’m not looking for an answer; I’m looking for someone to acknowledge that the *question* is worth asking.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is just how profoundly exhausting it is to constantly "edit" myself. We talk about the physical toll—the nausea, the exhaustion, the aches—but there is an entire, invisible layer of mental labor that’s just never addressed. It’s this endless, silent audit I’m running on my own pregnancy, day and night. 

I’m mourning the fact that I’ve spent the first trimester being a researcher rather than a mother-to-be. I wanted this to be a season of wonder, and instead, it’s become a season of risk-management. I wish there was more recognition that the "low-risk" label doesn't insulate us from the psychological weight of the journey. We are people who remember our losses, we are people who know our statistics, and we are people who are fundamentally changed by every single appointment. 

If there’s any takeaway for the research you’re doing, it’s this: the system is incredibly efficient at monitoring a uterus, but it is deeply, dangerously unequipped to support the person to whom that uterus belongs. I’m tired of being an "interesting case" or an "informed colleague" in the exam room. I just want to be seen, with all my baggage and all my fear, and told that I am doing a good job—not because the numbers are good, but because I am showing up, day after day, to fight for this life. Thank you for listening. It’s the first time I’ve felt like I could actually exhale in a very long time.
